condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I ran two research queries: one on **historical Johor turnout patterns** and one on **June–July 2026 campaign news and analyst turnout projections**. The research returned rich, well-sourced Johor-specific data.

The dominant historical pattern is **concurrent vs. standalone**: Johor ran ~84–85% in 2013 (with GE13) and **82.0%** in 2018 (with GE14), but collapsed to a record-low **54.92%** in the 2022 standalone snap poll — a 27-point drop driven by (1) the structural standalone gap, (2) Covid border closures blocking outstation/Singapore voters, and (3) Undi18 + automatic registration roll expansion (1.8M → 2.7M) mechanically lowering the percentage.

## Key Evidence

- **Johor-specific analyst consensus for 2026 clusters 60–70%**: UM's Tawfik "below 65%", UTM's Mazlan Ali "65–70%", UTHM's Md Akbal "at least 60%", Straits Times summary "likely below 65%". I centered this at **~64%**.
- **2026 context**: ~2.73M registered voters; 47.6% (~1.29M) under age 40; election is a **Saturday standalone poll**, no Covid restrictions; PH is subsidizing extra KL–Johor trains and expedited customs lanes to bring back Singapore-based voters.
- **Structural drag**: ~300k young Johor voters reside outside the state (mostly Singapore); ~400k daily Causeway crossings; younger rolls vote at lower rates; "ghost of 2022" apathy.
- **Non-Covid standalone comps** (recalled in code comments, not from research): Melaka 2021 **65.9%**, 2023 six-state polls **65–73%** — suggesting the analyst "below 65%" view may be slightly conservative.

## Conclusion

I formalized this as a **Monte Carlo scenario mixture** (200k samples): 50% base `N(64, 3.2)`, 27% apathy downside `N(58.5, 2.5)`, 23% youth-mobilization upside `N(70, 3.0)`. The 2022 low of 54.9% was treated as a Covid-depressed **downside tail only**, not part of the base.

Result: **median 63.7%**, P10 **57.4%**, P90 **70.8%**, mean 63.9%. My reflection anchored on the analyst consensus (~64%), and my premortem raised the strongest counterargument — that every recent non-Covid standalone ran ≥65%, so my distribution may be ~4pp too low — which I weighted but did not fully adopt (confidence 0.6), citing Johor's unique Singapore-commuter drag. The premortem reviewer independently verified the comps (Melaka 65.85%, 2023 six-state 65–73%) and approved with no material errors.

classification.approach: Monte Carlo scenario mixture (base/apathy-downside/youth-upside) anchored on Johor-specific analyst turnout projections and non-Covid Malaysian standalone state-election comps.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether Singapore-based/outstation voters actually return to vote — the single biggest swing factor for turnout.

risk_flags.evidence: The agent's own premortem noted every recent non-Covid standalone Malaysian state election ran >=65% (Melaka 65.9%, 2023 six-state 65-73%), plausibly implying a median of 68-72%, yet the base case was centered at 64% and the downside scenario (27% weight on N(58.5,2.5)) pulls the median below all these comps. The distribution may be shifted ~2-4pp low relative to the empirical non-Covid standalone base rate.

No prediction market (Polymarket/Kalshi/Manifold) or betting-odds check was attempted, and no July 7 advance-voting turnout figure was sought despite the agent identifying it as the key falsifier in the premortem (published July 1, advance poll July 7 was still future, so this is a minor gap).

strengths: Correctly identified the concurrent-vs-standalone structural distinction as the dominant driver, and deliberately excluded the Covid-depressed 2022 figure (54.9%) from the base case, using it only as a downside tail rather than as a naive base rate.
Grounded the base case in multiple named, attributed Johor-specific analyst forecasts (Tawfik, Mazlan Ali, Md Akbal, ST) rather than a generic prior.

Ran a genuine premortem that constructed a coherent, quantified alternative (analysts anchored on 2022; distribution ~4pp too low) with concrete falsifiers (advance-poll turnout, fresh Merdeka Centre projection, Causeway traffic reports).
Handled the Singapore-commuter and Undi18 roll-expansion factors as an explicit downward structural drag distinguishing Johor from other Malaysian states, avoiding naive comp transplantation.
weaknesses: Did not resolve the tension it surfaced in the premortem: the empirical non-Covid standalone base rate (>=65%) sits above the chosen median (63.7%), but the model retained a heavier downside than upside scenario weight (27% vs 23%) without a fully articulated justification beyond the Singapore drag.

The non-Covid comps (Melaka 2021, 2023 six-state) were recalled from memory in code comments rather than confirmed via the research tool; correct here (premortem verified them) but a fragile practice.
No sensitivity check on scenario weights was run to show how much the median moves if the apathy/upside weights are swapped.
actionable_improvements: When the premortem surfaces that the empirical base rate (>=65%) exceeds the chosen median, run a quick sensitivity check re-weighting the upside vs downside scenarios and report how far the median moves, rather than leaving the tension unresolved.
For election-turnout questions, check whether any prediction market or aggregator has a relevant contract, even if none is expected, to confirm the absence of market signal.
Confirm quantitative comps (Melaka 2021, 2023 six-state) via the research tool rather than relying on model memory in code comments, even when the recalled figures are correct.
